confidence.disclosure: Ibn Jubayr's first pilgrimage of 1183–1185 is the best-documented medieval Muslim journey we have. He kept a daily journal which became the Rihla, founding the genre of the Arabic travelogue. Almost every stop has a date — Granada 3 February 1183, Ceuta 24 February 1183, Mecca August 1183, Damascus mid-1184, Acre October 1184, return to Granada April 1185. The pre-1183 and post-1185 portions of his life are far less documented: we know he served as secretary to the governor of Granada and that he made two later pilgrimages, but the chronology is sparse. He died at Alexandria on 29 November 1217 on a third journey east. This file gives the famous first journey in detail and treats the rest more briefly.

sources: Ibn Jubayr, Rihla (The Travels), trans. R.J.C. Broadhurst (1952)
Charles Pellat, 'Ibn Djubayr' in Encyclopaedia of Islam
Ian Netton, Seek Knowledge: Thought and Travel in the House of Islam (1996)
Robert Irwin, 'Ibn Jubayr's Account' in The Cambridge History of Arabic Literature

journey.event: Born on 1 September 1145 in Valencia in al-Andalus, into an Arab family of the Kinanah tribe descended from a soldier who had come to Spain in 740 to put down a Berber uprising.

Serves as secretary to the Almohad governor of Granada, Abu Sa'id Uthman ibn Abd al-Mu'min. According to the famous tradition, the governor one day forces him to drink seven cups of wine, then in remorse fills the same cups with gold dinars; Ibn Jubayr decides to use the gold to perform the hajj as expiation.
On 3 February 1183 leaves Granada with his physician friend Ahmad ibn Hassan, beginning the journey that will become the first great rihla.

Crosses the Strait of Gibraltar to Ceuta in North Africa and on 24 February 1183 boards a Genoese ship bound for Alexandria. On the voyage he learns of eighty Muslim captives being shipped to be sold as slaves and writes about it with anger.
Disembarks at Alexandria, marvelling at the great lighthouse, which he describes from close inspection. Notes the harassment of pilgrims by Egyptian customs officials.

Reaches Cairo, then under the rule of Saladin, whose justice and generosity Ibn Jubayr praises throughout the Rihla. Visits the tombs of the Prophet's companions and writes admiring descriptions of the city's mosques, hospitals and madrasas.
Travels up the Nile by boat to Qus in Upper Egypt, then mounts a camel to ride east across the desert to the Red Sea port of Aydhab.
Crosses the Red Sea from Aydhab to Jeddah in a flimsy local boat. Describes the pearl fisheries of Aydhab and the appalling treatment of pilgrims by the local rulers, who tax them mercilessly.

Arrives at Mecca in August 1183 and stays for nine months. Performs the pilgrimage and observes the practices of the four schools of law together at the Kaaba — Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, Hanbali, and a fifth Zaydi imam. Notes that the Kaaba's covering at this time is green silk with a band of red.
Leaves Mecca on 15 March 1184 with the Iraqi pilgrim caravan and travels north to Medina to visit the Prophet's mosque.
Crosses the deserts of Najd in 'circular, even backward fashion', heading northeast across the Arabian peninsula toward Iraq. Reaches Kufa, the city of Imam Ali.

Reaches Baghdad, the seat of the Abbasid caliph al-Nasir. Praises 'the natural goodness of its air and waters' but is disappointed by the vanity and decadence of its inhabitants. Visits the tomb of al-Hallaj.
Travels north along the Tigris through Tikrit and Mosul, which Saladin had recently brought to terms.
Crosses into Syria, arriving in Aleppo, which Saladin had captured the previous year. Describes its prosperous markets and mixed inhabitants.
Reaches Damascus and spends several weeks praising the grandeur of the Umayyad Mosque and the fairness of Saladin's administration. He calls it the most beautiful city he has seen.

Crosses from Saladin's Syria into the Crusader Kingdom of Jerusalem. Famously describes Muslim peasants under Frankish rule as living more comfortably and paying lighter taxes than under their fellow Muslims — a passage that troubles his Maghrebi readers but which he records honestly.

Spends thirty-two days in the Crusader port of Acre, thirteen of them anchored in harbour waiting for a favourable wind. Describes the city as 'the meeting place of ships and caravans' but says its filth and Christian quarters fill it 'with pigs and crosses'.
From Acre travels along the coast to Tyre, also under Crusader rule, before boarding a Genoese ship for the homeward voyage.

His ship is wrecked off the coast of Sicily in a winter storm. He is rescued by King William II of Norman Sicily, who personally pays the ransoms for the Muslim passengers. Stays in Sicily for nearly four months and writes a long description of the hybrid Norman-Arab kingdom, where Muslims are still a numerous community.
Returns at last to Granada in April 1185 after more than two years away. Praises Allah abundantly. Writes up his journal as the Rihla, founding the genre.
Makes a second pilgrimage to Mecca and Medina, of which he leaves no detailed account.
Dies on 29 November 1217 in Alexandria, on his third pilgrimage east. He had ended his life as a teacher of hadith and Sufism in the city to which he had first sailed thirty-four years earlier.
